T1.label: Licensing Infrastructure
T1.tone: Senior, restrained, sector-specific. References style guides, licensee programmes, retail performance. No hard CTA before email 4.

steps.name: Credibility introduction
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026

steps.name: Trend brief follow-up
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

Following up on my note from a fortnight ago. Whether or not the trend brief itself is useful, the wider point I'd flag is this: the gap we keep seeing between licensing organisations and their licensees is creative direction at the category level — particularly apparel. Style guides and licensee packs are often produced as compliance documents rather than as tools that genuinely inspire great product, and the result is range inconsistency at retail.

That's the gap Studio IOS specialises in closing.

If trend direction or category-level apparel guidance is on your radar for {{company}}'s programme — current or upcoming season — I'd be glad to share what we've put together for similar mandates.

{{signature}}

steps.name: Case study, soft CTA
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

A short follow-up — no agenda.

When FUJI re-set their apparel programme we built the graphic language, trend framework and seasonal direction together with their commercial team. Two specifics that may be relevant to {{company}}:

1. The style-guide function shifted from compliance document to commercial tool. Licensees started using it as creative input, not just brand-protection input. Range coherence improved measurably across categories.
2. Trend intelligence was sequenced 9 months ahead of the buying cycle, which let category leads brief manufacturers off a single source of truth.

If either of those would be useful inside {{company}}'s programme — happy to walk through how we structured it. {{gdrive_link}} has the case study and the artefacts we shared with their team.

{{signature}}

steps.name: First soft CTA
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026

steps.name: Close the loop
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
Hi {{first_name}},

Closing this thread cleanly. If trend intelligence, apparel direction or style-guide capability becomes relevant for {{company}} in the future — whether that's a category expansion, a leadership change, or a programme refresh — my line is open.

Studio IOS sits in an unusually narrow niche: specialist creative direction for licensed apparel, with combat-sports and motorsport credibility most generalist agencies can't match. We're not for every programme, but we're a clean fit for the ones we are.

I'll come back if I see a reason that's specific to {{company}}. If you'd ever like to revisit before then, the door is open.

{{signature}}

T2.label: Growth-stage DTC
T2.tone: Direct, founder-to-founder, evidence-led. FUJI 15-18% uplift + Phalanx preorder-covers-fees. Seasonal-window urgency.

steps.name: Signal-led opener
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026

steps.name: Free trend deck offer
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

Following up. I can offer something useful regardless: the seasonal trend signals deck we're sharing with our Tier-2 brands this month — graphic language, colour, silhouette and category-momentum data drawn from what we're seeing land at retail.

It's the kind of thing most brands at your scale don't have access to. Free, no follow-up obligation. Reply with "send" and I'll fire it over.

{{signature}}

steps.name: Case study with numbers
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

Specifics:

— FUJI Sports: 17% YoY uplift on the season we directed. Trend signals locked 4 months ahead of the brief; collection structured around 3 graphic anchors instead of 9 disconnected ones.
— Phalanx: preorder revenue covered inventory + studio fees before launch. Achieved by tightening the graphic language to a single "buyable identity" the audience recognised instantly.

Same playbook works for {{company}} if {{custom_hook}}

20-min call to walk through what we'd build for your next collection? {{gdrive_link}} has both case studies in detail.

{{signature}}

steps.name: Direct window ask
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

Direct ask: when's your next collection briefing window?

If it's in the next 8 weeks, that's enough lead time for us to slot trend direction in before the brief lands at the manufacturer — which is where the seasonal uplift is unlocked.

If it's further out, no rush — drop me a note when it's on your radar and I'll prioritise the conversation.

{{signature}}

steps.name: Break-up, open door
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

Last touch from me — I won't keep nudging.

Two things on the way out:

1. If you do want the trend deck I mentioned, the offer stands indefinitely. Reply "send" any time.
2. If a season underperforms, or you find yourself reaching for trend intelligence you don't have, my line is open.

Best of luck with what's next at {{company}}.

{{signature}}

T3.label: Emerging founder brands
T3.tone: Personal, community-credibility-led, Instagram-DM informal. Accessible single-project entry point.

steps.name: Community-first opener
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

Bristol-based studio that lives in the BJJ / combat-sports space. I run Studio IOS — small team, deep in the culture. We've worked on FUJI and Phalanx, and a stack of independent brands across BJJ academies and athlete labels.

{{custom_hook}}

Most early-stage brands in our world hit the same wall: cheap creative makes the product impossible to price up. Distinctive creative is the only thing that lets a £55 rashguard actually sell at £55.

We run a starter graphic package specifically for brands at your stage — accessible price point, fast turnaround, designed to do one job: build product that travels beyond your immediate community.

Worth a chat?

{{signature}}

steps.name: Numbers + accessibility
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

Quick follow-up. Numbers from the FUJI work that I think matter if you're at the stage {{company}} is at:

— Sell-through on the graphics-led pieces was ~2x the plain-shell pieces.
— The graphic anchors we built are still appearing across their range a year later — meaning the design investment didn't depreciate after one drop.

That's the thing I'd want for {{company}}: graphics that work for the next four collections, not just the next one.

{{gdrive_link}} has the starter package format and what brands at your scale typically get from it.

{{signature}}

steps.name: Community / referral angle
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

The thing that actually makes brands at your stage grow is product community members are proud to wear and post in. That's marketing money you're not paying for — but only if the design earns it.

Generic Canva-grade design doesn't earn it. Cheap Fiverr work doesn't earn it. We've seen the difference inside the academies and athlete labels we've worked with — and the brands who invest at the right stage compound the advantage.

If a starter package is on the cards for {{company}}, I'd love to put one in front of you that's specific to where the brand is.

{{signature}}

steps.name: Direct simple ask
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

Easiest path forward: I scope a starter graphic package for {{company}} — what's in it, what it costs, what timing looks like — and you tell me yes, no, or "talk to me in 3 months."

Five-minute conversation, or a paragraph by email if that's easier. Up to you.

{{signature}}

steps.name: Break-up
steps.subject: {{company}} Creative 2026
steps.body:
{{first_name}},

I'll stop chasing — final note.

If {{company}}'s next product run feels like it deserves better creative than you're currently getting, my line is open. We work with academies and founder brands all the time, and most of them come back when the moment's right rather than the first time we speak.

Good luck with what's next.

{{signature}}
